title: Living with Understanding: The Duties of the Christian Husband
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 01-28-24 -AM- Living with Understanding - 1 Peter 3_7 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Peter Addresses Duties, Not Rights
subpoints: Peter addresses the wife's duties (vv. 1–6) and then the husband's duties (v. 7) — separately
He does not tell each party to demand that the other fulfill their duty
C.E.B. Cranfield: 'A marriage in which both partners are obsessed with their rights is hardly likely to prove very successful'

outline.title: 1. The Christian Husband Is Involved in His Own Marriage
subpoints: 'Dwell' = to live together in the same house, to do life together
This is an outworking of Genesis 2:24 — the man leaves and cleaves; they become one flesh
An absent husband cannot be faithful to God's calling
Question: Are you and your wife one, or do you live as roommates with separate lives?

outline.title: 2. The Christian Husband Studies His Wife to Know Her
subpoints: 'With understanding' = literally 'with knowledge' — a continual, ongoing action
This includes: knowledge of God's purpose for marriage, his wife's desires and frustrations, her strengths and weaknesses, his own duties as a husband
You cannot know your wife without spending time with her
Do you spend more time studying your hobbies or investments than you spend studying your wife?

outline.title: 3. The Christian Husband Honors His Wife
subpoints: Honor includes kind and affirming words privately and publicly, giving high priority in the use of money and time
He honors what is good and godly in her — love must never become an excuse for enabling sin (cf. Solomon, Ahab, Adam)
Peter uses a rare word for 'wife' meaning 'female' or 'feminine' — implying that her womanhood itself is worthy of honor
'Weaker vessel': not implying inferiority but difference — men must not misuse their strength or authority
'Joint heirs of the grace of life': your wife is equally a child of God and heir of Christ — different roles do not mean different worth

outline.title: 4. The Christian Husband Prays Without Hindrance
subpoints: God ties the husband's prayer life to his treatment of his wife
If the husband is not living with understanding and honoring his wife, his prayers are hindered
No amount of Bible reading, church attendance, or spiritual gifting compensates for this failure
The call to be a godly husband is itself a call to a deeper relationship with God through prayer

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro expounds 1 Peter 3:7, the single verse addressed to husbands in Peter's passage on submission. He argues that Peter addresses duties rather than rights, and draws out four characteristics of the Christian husband: he is genuinely involved in his marriage (dwelling together), he studies his wife to truly know her (living with knowledge), he honors her both as the weaker vessel and as a joint heir of grace, and he prays without hindrance — a privilege that God withholds from the husband who neglects his wife.
transcript:
## Introduction: One Verse for the Husband

Just one verse — that is all we are going to consider today. Some commentators make much of the difference in length between Peter's instruction to wives (six verses) and his instruction to husbands (one verse). Some say wives have a harder task and need more instruction; others say there were more women in Peter's congregations. I do not think any such explanation is needed. The Holy Spirit thought it wise to give us this verse, and it is what a Christian husband needs to hear. Do not let the brevity deceive you into thinking it is less important.

## Peter Addresses Duties, Not Rights

Notice what Peter does not do. He does not tell the husband to demand from the wife that she do her duty. And he does not tell the wife to demand from her husband that he do his. The scholar C.E.B. Cranfield puts it this way:

> "Peter is all the time talking about duties rather than rights. Both the wives and the husbands are told what they are to give, not what they are to claim... A marriage in which both partners are obsessed with their rights is hardly likely to prove very successful."

In His wisdom, God addresses the husband about his duties, addresses the wife about her duties, and allows each to hear what the other's duties are — so that each can make it easier for the other to fulfill them.

## Four Things Peter Teaches About the Christian Husband

### 1. The Christian Husband Is Involved in His Own Marriage

> "Husbands, likewise, dwell with them..." (1 Peter 3:7)

The word *dwell* means to live together in the same house — to do life together. It is the outworking of Genesis 2:24: the man leaves his father and mother, cleaves to his wife, and the two become one flesh.

A husband cannot be faithful to God's calling if he is absent. And being absent is more than just not being physically present. Brother husband: are you doing life together with your wife? Is your life with your wife one life? Or are you roommates who share an address but have separate worlds?

This responsibility rests on your shoulders. If your life with your wife is divergent, it is your responsibility to do something about it.

### 2. The Christian Husband Studies His Wife to Know Her

> "...dwell with them with understanding..." (1 Peter 3:7)

Literally: *with knowledge*. The verb form indicates a continual, ongoing action. Brother husband, do you study your wife?

This knowledge includes:
- What marriage is and should be according to God's Word
- Your wife's desires, goals, and frustrations
- Her strengths and weaknesses — physically, emotionally, spiritually
- Your own duties as a husband

You cannot know your wife without spending time with her. Do you spend more time and effort studying your sports team, your investments, or your hobbies than you spend studying your wife? God calls you to continually learn more about her.

### 3. The Christian Husband Honors His Wife

> "...giving honor to the wife..." (1 Peter 3:7)

Honor is more than courtesy. It includes affirming words in private and in public, and giving your wife high priority in how you use your money and time.

Peter uses a rare word here for "wife" that actually means *female* or *feminine*, suggesting that her womanhood itself — the ways she is different from you — is worthy of honor.

Peter gives two reasons to honor her:

**As the weaker vessel.** This does not mean inferior, but different. By and large, women are physically weaker than men. There is also a difference in authority within marriage. Peter tells husbands: do not misuse your physical strength or your authority to take advantage of her. Honor her weaknesses by not exploiting them. Be gentle and positive rather than harsh and critical.

Biblical illustrations of men who failed here: Solomon, who allowed love for his many wives to lead him into idolatry; Ahab, who let Jezebel lead him into sin; and Adam, who stood by as his wife was deceived and did not intervene.

**As joint heir of the grace of life.** Your Christian wife has been redeemed by the same blood of Christ as you. The Holy Spirit has given her the same new heart. She has been adopted by the same Father. She is a daughter of the King, loved by the Father, and heir of Christ — equally with you. Differing roles do not result in differences of worth.

### 4. The Christian Husband Prays Without Hindrance

> "...that your prayers may not be hindered." (1 Peter 3:7)

God is so concerned that Christian husbands live with understanding and love toward their wives that He ties the husband's prayer life to his marriage. If the husband is failing in his duties as a husband, his prayers are hindered.

No Christian husband should presume that spiritual good will be accomplished through a prayer life that is compromised by a neglected marriage. It does not matter how many chapters of the Bible you read, how often you attend church, or how gifted you are. If you are not living with understanding and honoring your wife, you are not pleasing God.

## Christ's Grace Is Sufficient

At this point you may be asking: who is sufficient for these things? The answer is Christ and His grace. He promised to equip you with all that you need to be a godly husband. Let us repent for not coming to Him for that grace, and commit to keep returning to the fountain of never-ending grace that is the Lord Jesus Christ.
